source: Based on Manchester Triage System (MTS), Emergency Severity Index (ESI v4), WHO Emergency Care guidelines, and ICD-10-CM clinical definitions.

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with crushing or pressure-like chest pain radiating to the left arm or jaw, accompanied by diaphoresis and shortness of breath.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: High probability of acute cardiac event (STEMI/NSTEMI). Symptom triad of chest pain, diaphoresis, and referred arm/jaw pain is classic MI presentation. Time-to-reperfusion is the primary determinant of myocardial salvage — every minute delays increases infarct size.
clarifying_questions: Did the pain start suddenly or build up gradually?
Have you taken any erectile dysfunction medication (like Viagra or Cialis) in the last 48 hours?
Does the pain get worse when you press on your chest, take a deep breath, or lie flat?
next_actions: Administer 12-lead ECG immediately (within 10 minutes of arrival)
Activate cath lab / call cardiologist on duty if STEMI confirmed
Administer Aspirin 300mg PO if not contraindicated
Establish IV access — draw troponin, CBC, BMP, coagulation panel
Continuous cardiac monitoring + pulse oximetry
Prepare for possible primary PCI or thrombolysis
contraindications: Do NOT administer Nitroglycerin if systolic BP < 90 mmHg
Do NOT administer Nitroglycerin if patient has taken phosphodiesterase inhibitors (sildenafil/tadalafil) within 24-48 hours
Avoid Aspirin if known hypersensitivity or active GI bleed

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with acute shortness of breath, audible wheezing, inability to speak in full sentences, accessory muscle use, and possible cyanosis.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Acute respiratory compromise with hypoxia risk. Causes include severe asthma exacerbation, COPD exacerbation, pulmonary embolism, tension pneumothorax, or acute pulmonary oedema. Silent chest in an asthmatic is an ominous sign — indicates airways too constricted to generate wheeze.
clarifying_questions: Are you able to speak in full sentences right now?
Do you have a history of asthma, COPD, or heart failure?
Have you been bedridden recently or had any recent surgeries?
next_actions: Apply high-flow oxygen (15L via non-rebreather mask) immediately
Nebulised salbutamol (2.5-5mg) + ipratropium bromide (0.5mg)
IV/IM corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 200mg or methylprednisolone 125mg)
Obtain arterial blood gas (ABG) urgently
Prepare for possible BiPAP/intubation if no improvement in 15-20 min
Call senior physician / anaesthetics team
contraindications: Avoid high-flow oxygen in known COPD patients — target SpO2 88-92% to prevent hypercapnia suppression
Do not use IV beta-blockers — may worsen bronchospasm

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with sudden unilateral facial droop, arm weakness, slurred or absent speech — classic FAST triad. May include sudden severe headache ('thunderclap') or visual loss.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Time-critical neurological emergency. Ischaemic stroke: 'time is brain' — approximately 1.9 million neurones die per minute without treatment. IV tPA must be administered within 4.5 hours of symptom onset. Haemorrhagic stroke requires urgent neurosurgical consultation. TIA is a harbinger of imminent stroke — treat as emergency.
clarifying_questions: When exactly did these symptoms first start (last known normal time)?
Are you currently taking any blood thinners like Warfarin or Eliquis?
Can you smile evenly and raise both arms without one drifting down?
next_actions: Activate stroke code / call neurology team immediately
Non-contrast CT head STAT (within 25 minutes of arrival)
Note exact time of symptom onset — critical for thrombolysis eligibility
Establish IV access — glucose, CBC, coagulation, type and screen
Do NOT lower blood pressure unless > 220/120 or haemorrhage confirmed
NPO (nil by mouth) until swallow assessment completed
contraindications: Do NOT administer Aspirin until haemorrhagic stroke is excluded by CT
Do NOT aggressively lower BP in ischaemic stroke — permissive hypertension is required

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with sudden onset urticaria, throat swelling/stridor, hypotension, and bronchospasm following exposure to an allergen (food, drug, insect sting).
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Life-threatening systemic hypersensitivity reaction. Airway compromise and cardiovascular collapse can occur within minutes. Epinephrine is the only definitive first-line treatment — antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts, NOT substitutes. Biphasic anaphylaxis can occur 1-72 hours after initial episode.
clarifying_questions: Do you feel like your throat is closing, or are you having trouble swallowing?
Were you recently stung by an insect, or did you eat a known allergen or take a new medication?
Do you have an EpiPen with you right now?
next_actions: Administer Epinephrine (adrenaline) 0.5mg IM (1:1000) in the anterolateral thigh — FIRST LINE
Call for resuscitation team / senior physician immediately
Position patient supine with legs raised (unless respiratory distress — sit upright)
High-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask
IV access — 500-1000mL rapid IV fluid bolus (crystalloid) if hypotension
Chlorphenamine 10mg IV + Hydrocortisone 200mg IV (after epinephrine)
Admit for minimum 6-hour observation (biphasic risk)
contraindications: Do NOT delay Epinephrine to administer antihistamines first
Do NOT use oral antihistamines as sole treatment for anaphylaxis

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with fever or hypothermia, altered mental status, tachycardia, and hypotension unresponsive to initial fluid resuscitation, with suspected infection source.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Septic shock is life-organ threatening. Defined as sepsis with persisting hypotension (MAP < 65 mmHg) despite adequate fluid resuscitation, requiring vasopressors. Lactate > 2 mmol/L suggests tissue hypoperfusion. Mortality increases ~8% per hour without antibiotics. Follow Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1 Bundle.
clarifying_questions: Have you recently had a medical procedure, surgery, or been treated for an infection (like a UTI or pneumonia)?
Are you experiencing severe, uncontrollable shaking or chills?
Has the patient seemed unusually confused, drowsy, or difficult to wake up today?
next_actions: Draw blood cultures x2 sets BEFORE administering antibiotics
Administer broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour (e.g. Piperacillin-Tazobactam + Vancomycin)
IV fluid resuscitation: 30mL/kg crystalloid within 3 hours
Check serum lactate — if > 4 mmol/L, immediate ICU alert
Insert urinary catheter — target urine output > 0.5 mL/kg/hr
Start vasopressors (Norepinephrine) if MAP < 65 despite fluids
Alert ICU / senior physician immediately
contraindications: Avoid hypotonic IV fluids (D5W) for resuscitation
Avoid corticosteroids unless refractory shock to vasopressors

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with severely elevated blood pressure (typically > 180/120 mmHg) with evidence of acute end-organ damage: confusion, visual changes, chest pain, or pulmonary oedema.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Hypertensive emergency (vs urgency) requires evidence of acute target organ damage. Organs at risk: brain (hypertensive encephalopathy, stroke), heart (ACS, aortic dissection), kidneys (acute kidney injury), eyes (papilloedema). Goal is controlled BP reduction — usually 10-20% within first hour, not to normal.
clarifying_questions: Are you experiencing any chest pain, shortness of breath, or back pain right now?
Have you noticed any sudden blurriness or loss of vision?
Do you have a history of high blood pressure, and have you missed any doses of your medication?
next_actions: Continuous BP monitoring (every 5-15 minutes initially)
IV antihypertensive therapy (Labetalol, Nicardipine, or Clevidipine)
ECG, chest X-ray, head CT if neurological symptoms
Renal function panel (creatinine, BUN) + urinalysis
Ophthalmology review if visual symptoms (fundoscopy for papilloedema)
Cardiac monitoring and IV access
contraindications: Do NOT lower BP too rapidly — risk of cerebral or coronary ischaemia
Avoid sublingual Nifedipine — unpredictable rapid BP drop can be dangerous
Avoid ACE inhibitors in bilateral renal artery stenosis

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with high fever (> 38.5°C), chills/rigors, focal infection signs (e.g. productive cough, dysuria, neck stiffness), and possible confusion in elderly patients.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: High fever with rigors suggests bacteraemia. In elderly patients, confusion may be the only presenting symptom of serious infection. Fever > 40°C risks febrile seizures in children. Neck stiffness + photophobia = meningism until proven otherwise — requires immediate LP consideration.
clarifying_questions: Does your neck feel unusually stiff, or does looking at bright lights hurt your eyes?
Have you traveled outside the country recently?
Do you have a new, persistent cough, or any pain when you urinate?
next_actions: Full sepsis screen: Blood cultures x2, FBC, CRP, procalcitonin, LFTs, U&E
Urinalysis + MSU culture
Chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms
Paracetamol/Ibuprofen for fever management
IV fluids if signs of dehydration or haemodynamic compromise
Assess for meningism — if suspected, CT head then LP
Empirical antibiotics if source identified or sepsis suspected
contraindications: Avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment or history of GI bleed
Do NOT prescribe antibiotics without cultures if clinically stable — allows targeted therapy

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents following injury with suspected fractures, significant soft-tissue injury, brief loss of consciousness (< 5 minutes), or blunt abdominal trauma.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Moderate trauma requires systematic assessment using ATLS (Advanced Trauma Life Support) principles. Occult injuries (splenic or hepatic laceration, pneumothorax) may be missed without imaging. Any head trauma with brief LOC, post-traumatic amnesia, or vomiting warrants CT head.
clarifying_questions: Did you lose consciousness or black out, even for a few seconds?
Are you currently taking any blood-thinning medications?
Are you experiencing any numbness, tingling, or inability to move your fingers or toes?
next_actions: Cervical spine immobilisation if mechanism suggests spinal injury
Primary survey: Airway, Breathing, Circulation (ABCDE)
CT head if LOC, amnesia, GCS < 15, or significant mechanism
X-rays of suspected fracture sites
IV access + FBC, group and screen (if bleeding suspected)
Pain management with IV/IM analgesia
Tetanus prophylaxis if open wound
contraindications: Do NOT move patient without cervical spine clearance if high-velocity trauma
Avoid NSAIDs if internal bleeding suspected

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with acute localised abdominal pain — particularly right iliac fossa (appendicitis), periumbilical, or generalised with guarding and rigidity suggesting peritonitis.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Acute abdomen may represent surgical emergency: appendicitis (perforation risk within 24-72 hours), bowel obstruction, mesenteric ischaemia, or perforated viscus. Peritonism indicates contamination of the peritoneum and potential sepsis. Never give analgesia before surgical assessment in classical teaching — this is now outdated; adequate analgesia does not mask signs.
clarifying_questions: Did the pain start around your belly button and move to the lower right side of your stomach?
Is there any chance you could be pregnant right now?
Does the pain get sharply worse when you cough, jump, or hit a bump in the road?
next_actions: Surgical team review — assess for peritonism (rebound tenderness, guarding)
FBC, CRP, LFTs, amylase/lipase, U&E, group and screen
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (gold standard for acute abdomen)
Erect CXR (to exclude free air under diaphragm)
IV fluid resuscitation if dehydrated/haemodynamically compromised
IV analgesia (Morphine + Ondansetron for nausea)
NPO — prepare for possible emergency surgery
contraindications: Do NOT give oral fluids or food if surgical intervention is planned
Avoid morphine if allergy — use fentanyl or hydromorphone

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with altered consciousness, diaphoresis, and tremors (hypoglycaemia) — or polyuria, polydipsia, vomiting, and fruity breath (DKA). Blood glucose is the key differentiator.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Two distinct emergencies: (1) Hypoglycaemia (glucose < 3.5 mmol/L) — rapid deterioration to seizures and coma, easily reversed but dangerous if missed. (2) Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA — glucose > 11 mmol/L + ketones > 3 mmol/L + acidosis pH < 7.3) — requires structured IV fluid and insulin protocol. DKA can be first presentation of Type 1 DM.
clarifying_questions: When was the last time you ate or took your insulin/diabetes medication?
Have you been urinating much more frequently or feeling extremely thirsty today?
Have you recently been sick with a cold, infection, or stomach bug?
next_actions: Capillary blood glucose IMMEDIATELY (point-of-care)
If glucose < 3.5 mmol/L: oral glucose gel if conscious, or IV 50% dextrose if not
If DKA suspected: VBG (pH, bicarb, ketones), urinalysis (ketones)
IV access — FBC, U&E, glucose, ketones, blood cultures
IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl 1L over 1 hour for DKA
Fixed-rate insulin infusion (0.1 units/kg/hr) once potassium confirmed > 3.5
Hourly monitoring of glucose, ketones, electrolytes
contraindications: Do NOT start insulin in DKA if potassium < 3.5 mmol/L — risk of lethal hypokalaemia
Do NOT give oral glucose to unconscious patient — aspiration risk

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with palpitations, irregular heartbeat, syncope or near-syncope, and possible chest discomfort — particularly if haemodynamically unstable.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Haemodynamically significant arrhythmias (AF with RVR, SVT, VT, heart block) require urgent evaluation. Syncope with palpitations suggests a cardiac cause. Key question: is the patient haemodynamically stable? Unstable (BP < 90, altered consciousness, chest pain) = immediate DC cardioversion. AF management depends on duration of onset (< 48hr vs > 48hr) due to thrombus risk.
clarifying_questions: Do you feel like you are going to pass out right now, or have you already fainted?
Are you experiencing any chest pain or shortness of breath with these palpitations?
Has this happened to you before, and if so, how long has it been going on this time?
next_actions: 12-lead ECG immediately and rhythm strip
Cardiac monitoring — continuous pulse oximetry
If unstable: prepare for synchronised DC cardioversion
If stable AF < 48 hrs: rate control (Metoprolol IV or Digoxin)
If stable SVT: Vagal manoeuvres, then Adenosine 6mg IV rapid push
U&E (check potassium + magnesium), TFTs, FBC
Echocardiogram if structural heart disease suspected
contraindications: Do NOT use Adenosine in Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) — may precipitate VF
Avoid Verapamil in SVT if pre-excited QRS — can cause VF
Do NOT cardiovert AF > 48 hours duration without anticoagulation or TOE clearance — stroke risk

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with suicidal ideation, self-harm, acute psychosis (hallucinations/delusions), severe agitation, or inability to maintain personal safety.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Mental health crises can be life-threatening. Active suicidal ideation with a plan and means available is a psychiatric emergency. Acute psychosis may indicate first episode schizophrenia, drug-induced psychosis, organic cause (encephalitis, metabolic), or acute mania. Always exclude organic causes (hypoglycaemia, head injury, intoxication) before attributing to psychiatric cause.
clarifying_questions: Are you currently having thoughts of hurting yourself or ending your life, and do you have a plan?
Have you consumed any drugs, alcohol, or taken any medications today?
Are you seeing or hearing things that other people do not seem to notice?
next_actions: Ensure patient and staff safety — de-escalation as first approach
Risk assessment: suicidal intent, plan, means, protective factors
Psychiatric team referral / crisis team notification
Medical exclusion: glucose, TFTs, FBC, U&E, drug screen, CT head if warranted
Oral benzodiazepine if severe agitation (Lorazepam 1-2mg PO)
IM sedation if rapid tranquilisation needed (Olanzapine 10mg IM or Haloperidol 5mg IM)
Do NOT leave patient unattended — 1:1 observation
contraindications: Avoid IM Olanzapine within 1 hour of IM Lorazepam — risk of respiratory depression
Do NOT restrain physically without clinical justification and documentation

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with sudden onset dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, and tachycardia — often following immobilisation, recent surgery, or DVT.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Pulmonary embolism is a potentially fatal obstruction of the pulmonary arterial system. Massive PE (haemodynamic instability) requires immediate systemic thrombolysis unless contraindicated. Use Wells PE Score to estimate pre-test probability. D-dimer is only useful if pre-test probability is low — do not use to exclude PE in high-probability cases. CT Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA) is gold standard.
clarifying_questions: Have you recently had surgery, been bedridden, or been on a long flight?
Are you coughing up any blood?
Do you have any pain, swelling, or redness in one of your legs?
next_actions: High-flow oxygen and continuous monitoring
Calculate Wells PE Score — if high probability, proceed to CTPA immediately
If haemodynamically unstable: systemic thrombolysis (Alteplase 100mg IV over 2hrs) or surgical embolectomy
CTPA (CT Pulmonary Angiography) — gold standard imaging
D-dimer only if low pre-test probability Wells score
Anticoagulation: LMWH/UFH once PE confirmed (or high suspicion with no haemorrhage risk)
ICU admission for massive PE
contraindications: Avoid thrombolysis if recent surgery, stroke, or active internal bleeding
Do NOT rely on D-dimer alone to exclude PE in high-probability patients

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with diffuse or colicky abdominal pain without signs of peritonism, associated with nausea, vomiting, or changes in bowel habit.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Moderate abdominal pain requires location-based assessment: RUQ = biliary/hepatic; LUQ = splenic/gastric; RIF = appendicitis/ovarian; LIF = diverticulitis; flank = renal colic; periumbilical = early appendicitis/mesenteric. No peritonism signs and haemodynamically stable = MODERATE urgency. Beware of atypical presentations in elderly and diabetic patients.
clarifying_questions: Does the pain shoot around to your back, flank, or shoulder?
Have you noticed any blood in your vomit or your stool?
When was your last bowel movement, and was it normal for you?
next_actions: Abdominal examination: location, character, radiation, guarding, bowel sounds
FBC, CRP, U&E, LFTs, amylase if epigastric pain
Urine pregnancy test in all females of reproductive age
Urinalysis (exclude UTI/renal colic)
Ultrasound abdomen (first line for RUQ pain, pelvic pain)
IV/PO analgesia as appropriate
Dietary advice and early review
contraindications: Do NOT use NSAIDs if peptic ulcer history or suspected renal colic without adequate hydration

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with dysuria, frequency, and urgency (lower UTI) — or fever, rigors, and flank pain suggesting pyelonephritis (upper UTI).
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Pyelonephritis (upper UTI with flank pain, fever, rigors) requires IV antibiotics and admission if severe. Simple lower UTI in young healthy females can be treated with oral antibiotics in community. Complicated UTI features: male sex, pregnancy, catheterised, structural urinary abnormality, immunosuppression, or failure of prior treatment.
clarifying_questions: Are you experiencing any fever, severe chills, or pain in your mid-to-lower back?
Is there any visible blood in your urine, or does it smell unusually strong?
Do you currently have a urinary catheter, or have you had one recently?
next_actions: Urinalysis + MSU for culture and sensitivity before antibiotics
Blood cultures if fever/systemic sepsis features present
U&E (renal function) + FBC + CRP
Ultrasound kidneys if obstruction suspected or no improvement
IV antibiotics for pyelonephritis (Ceftriaxone 1-2g IV)
Oral antibiotics for simple lower UTI (Trimethoprim / Nitrofurantoin)
Adequate IV/PO hydration
contraindications: Avoid Nitrofurantoin if eGFR < 45 (ineffective and risk of pulmonary toxicity)
Avoid Trimethoprim in pregnancy (folate antagonism in first trimester)

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with localised joint or limb pain, swelling, restricted movement, and possible bruising following trauma or overuse.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Musculoskeletal injuries are common and rarely immediately life-threatening. Key distinction: fracture vs sprain vs tendon injury. Ottawa rules guide X-ray decisions for ankle and knee injuries. Compartment syndrome is a rare but limb-threatening complication of severe closed injuries — suspect if pain out of proportion, pallor, paraesthesia, paralysis.
clarifying_questions: Are you able to put any weight on the injured area or move the joint at all?
Is there any numbness, tingling, or 'pins and needles' feeling below the injury?
Did you hear a 'pop' or 'snap' when the injury happened?
next_actions: Clinical assessment: neurovascular status distal to injury (pulse, sensation, movement)
Apply Ottawa Rules to determine need for X-ray
X-ray of affected area if fracture suspected
RICE: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation
Oral analgesia: Paracetamol + Ibuprofen (if no contraindications)
Immobilisation (splint/cast) if fracture confirmed
Orthopaedic referral for complex fractures or compartment syndrome suspicion
contraindications: Do NOT splint in circumferential plaster if acute swelling — risk of compartment syndrome
Avoid weight-bearing until fracture excluded

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with sore throat, runny nose, mild cough, low-grade fever, and general malaise consistent with viral upper respiratory tract infection.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Vast majority are viral (rhinovirus, adenovirus, influenza) and self-limiting within 7-10 days. Antibiotics are NOT indicated for viral URTI. Centor/McIsaac criteria help identify bacterial pharyngitis (Streptococcal) warranting antibiotics: fever, exudate, tender LAP, no cough (3-4 = treat empirically or swab). Complications: sinusitis, otitis media, peritonsillar abscess.
clarifying_questions: Do you have a very high fever, or is it mostly just a sore throat and runny nose?
Are you having any difficulty breathing, or struggling to swallow your own saliva?
Have these symptoms been getting progressively worse over the last week?
next_actions: Assess Centor/McIsaac Score for bacterial pharyngitis
Throat swab for culture if Centor score 3-4
Supportive management: rest, hydration, paracetamol/ibuprofen
Antibiotics ONLY if bacterial source confirmed (Phenoxymethylpenicillin for strep)
Educate on self-care and return precautions (worsening, difficulty breathing, drooling)
Advise 48-72 hour watchful waiting before antibiotic consideration
contraindications: Do NOT prescribe antibiotics for viral URTI — antimicrobial stewardship
Avoid Aspirin in children under 16 — Reye syndrome risk

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with reduced urine output, dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, and fatigue following vomiting, diarrhoea, or inadequate fluid intake.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Moderate dehydration (5-10% body weight fluid loss) presents with tachycardia, reduced skin turgor, dry mucous membranes. Severe dehydration (>10%) shows hypotension, altered consciousness, and requires IV resuscitation. Electrolyte imbalance (hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia) are common complications of vomiting/diarrhoea. Infants and elderly are most vulnerable.
clarifying_questions: Are you able to keep any sips of water down, or are you vomiting everything immediately?
When was the last time you urinated, and was it very dark in color?
Have you had severe, watery diarrhea for more than 24 hours?
next_actions: Assess degree of dehydration (mild/moderate/severe)
U&E + glucose (electrolyte imbalance common with vomiting/diarrhoea)
Oral rehydration salts (ORS) if able to tolerate
IV fluids if severe dehydration or unable to tolerate PO (0.9% NaCl with KCl supplementation)
Anti-emetics (Ondansetron 4mg PO/IV) to enable oral hydration
Stool culture if infective diarrhoea suspected
Monitor urine output and electrolytes
contraindications: Avoid hypotonic fluids (D5W alone) in severe dehydration
Avoid anti-diarrhoeals (Loperamide) in bloody diarrhoea / infective colitis

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with localised rash, urticaria, mild contact dermatitis, or eczema flare — without systemic symptoms or airway involvement.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Minor skin conditions are generally not life-threatening but cause significant discomfort. Key distinction: rule out anaphylaxis (no throat swelling, no hypotension, no bronchospasm). Simple urticaria without systemic features = LOW urgency. Contact dermatitis: identify and remove allergen. Target lesions suggest erythema multiforme — possible drug reaction.
clarifying_questions: Is the rash spreading very rapidly, or are there any large blisters forming?
Are you having any trouble breathing, or does your throat feel tight?
Have you started any new medications or eaten anything unusual recently?
next_actions: Confirm no airway involvement (throat inspection), no systemic symptoms
Identify potential trigger (contact, food, drug, insect)
Oral antihistamine (Cetirizine 10mg or Chlorphenamine 4mg) for urticaria
Topical corticosteroid (Hydrocortisone 1%) for localised eczema/contact dermatitis
Advise to return immediately if throat swelling, dyspnoea, or collapse
Refer to dermatology or allergy clinic if recurrent
contraindications: Do NOT prescribe systemic steroids for routine urticaria unless severe
Avoid sedating antihistamines in elderly — fall risk

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with lumbar back pain, typically after lifting or prolonged posture, without neurological deficits or red flag features.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Non-specific low back pain is the most common musculoskeletal complaint. Vast majority resolve within 6 weeks. Screen for RED FLAGS: saddle anaesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction (cauda equina — emergency MRI), bilateral leg weakness, unexplained weight loss (malignancy), fever with back pain (spinal infection), age < 20 or > 50 with new onset (fracture/malignancy).
clarifying_questions: Are you experiencing any loss of bowel or bladder control, or numbness in your groin/saddle area?
Does the pain shoot sharply down your leg, past your knee, into your foot?
Do you have a fever, or have you recently had unexplained weight loss?
next_actions: Screen for red flags: saddle anaesthesia, bowel/bladder symptoms, bilateral limb weakness
Neurological examination: power, reflexes, sensation in legs
If no red flags: reassure, analgesia (Paracetamol + low-dose Ibuprofen)
Encourage gentle mobilisation — avoid prolonged bed rest
Physiotherapy referral for exercises
X-ray only if red flags present or trauma mechanism
MRI if cauda equina suspected — emergency
contraindications: Do NOT prescribe opioids as first-line for acute low back pain
Avoid prolonged bed rest — worsens outcomes

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with sudden intense fear, palpitations, hyperventilation, chest tightness, and tingling — typically in a young adult with no prior cardiac history.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Panic disorder is a diagnosis of exclusion — must rule out cardiac arrhythmia, pulmonary embolism, and hyperthyroidism first, especially in age > 40 or first episode. Hyperventilation causes hypocapnia → respiratory alkalosis → peripheral paraesthesia (tingling hands, perioral). These symptoms mimic serious pathology but are benign. Treatment: reassurance, controlled breathing, treat underlying anxiety.
clarifying_questions: Have you ever had a panic attack that felt exactly like this before?
Are you feeling a numbness or tingling sensation in your fingers or around your mouth?
Do you have any history of heart problems, or is this the very first time you have felt this way?
next_actions: Rule out organic causes: 12-lead ECG, SpO2, blood glucose, capillary gas if hyperventilating
Blood tests: TFTs (thyrotoxicosis exclusion), FBC, U&E
Reassurance and calm explanation of symptoms
Controlled breathing technique: 4-7-8 or box breathing
Benzodiazepine (Lorazepam 1mg PO) only if first episode or severe distress — not for repeated use
Refer to psychology/CBT or psychiatry for recurrent panic disorder
Safety netting: advise to return if chest pain worsens, syncope, or new symptoms
contraindications: Do NOT prescribe benzodiazepines regularly — dependence risk
Do NOT dismiss without performing ECG in first episode, age > 35, or risk factors

symptom_clusters.presentation_summary: Patient presents with mild nausea, indigestion, heartburn, or transient vomiting without systemic signs, significant pain, or blood in vomit.
symptom_clusters.clinical_context: Functional dyspepsia and mild gastroenteritis are extremely common and self-limiting. Red flags that upgrade urgency: haematemesis, melaena, significant weight loss, dysphagia, persistent vomiting > 24 hours, or signs of dehydration. Heartburn (GERD) is most common cause of epigastric discomfort in the community — responds to PPIs.
clarifying_questions: Have you vomited anything that looks like dark coffee grounds, or noticed black, tarry stools?
Are you able to drink small sips of water without immediately throwing them back up?
Does the discomfort feel more like a burning sensation behind your breastbone after eating?
next_actions: Screen for red flags: blood in vomit/stool, weight loss, dysphagia, age > 55 new onset
Dietary advice: avoid triggers (spicy food, caffeine, alcohol, lying flat post meals)
Antacid (Gaviscon) or PPI (Omeprazole 20mg OD) for GERD/dyspepsia
Anti-emetic (Metoclopramide 10mg PO) for nausea
Adequate hydration — ORS if vomiting
Return if symptoms worsen, persist > 3 days, or if blood in vomit/stool
contraindications: Avoid Metoclopramide in Parkinson's disease or tardive dyskinesia history
Do NOT use Aspirin/NSAIDs for perceived stomach pain — may worsen GI bleeding
